title: Non-integer surgery and branched double covers of alternating knots
overall_summary: The paper proves that when the branched double cover of an alternating link is obtained by non-integer surgery of slope p/q with |p/q| > 1, the surgery is realized diagrammatically by a rational tangle replacement in an alternating diagram. The core method combines Heegaard Floer correction-term surgery obstructions, Donaldson-type lattice embedding input, and a detailed structural analysis of Goeritz graph lattices that are isomorphic to p/q-changemaker lattices. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the purely lattice-theoretic material: graph-lattice irreducibility criteria, the behavior of irreducible elements under decompositions, and changemaker-lattice arguments certifying irreducibility from coordinate patterns. Especially useful are Lemmas 3.1–3.4 and 6.5–6.8, which give reusable mechanisms for proving that certain elements cannot split as a sum with nonnegative inner product.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3 graph-lattice setup and identities
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G = (V,E) be a finite connected undirected graph with no self-loops. For disjoint subsets R,S ⊆ V, let e(R,S) be the number of edges between R and S, and let d(R) = e(R,V \ R). Let Λbar(G) be the free abelian group generated by V, with symmetric bilinear form defined on vertices by v·w = d(v) if v = w and v·w = −e(v,w) if v ≠ w. For R ⊆ V, write [R] = ∑_{v∈R} v. Then v·[R] = −e(v,R) if v ∉ R, and v·[R] = e(v,V \ R) if v ∈ R. In particular, [V]·x = 0 for all x ∈ Λbar(G), so the form descends to the quotient graph lattice Λ(G) = Λbar(G) / Z[V]. Since G is connected, the descended form on Λ(G) is positive definite.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The displayed formula for v·[R] is obtained by summing v·u over all u ∈ R: if v ∉ R only off-diagonal edge terms occur, while if v ∈ R the diagonal d(v) is reduced by the number of edges from v to other vertices of R, leaving the number of edges from v to V \ R. The identity [V]·x = 0 follows by taking R = V. Positive definiteness is the usual Laplacian energy calculation: for a representative ∑ a_v v, the square is a sum over edges of terms of the form (a_u − a_v)^2, whose kernel consists exactly of constant functions when G is connected; quotienting by Z[V] removes this kernel.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the graph lattice Λ(G) of a finite connected graph G as above, if x = [R] is the class of a sum of vertices over some subset R ⊆ V, then for every z ∈ Λ(G) one has (x − z)·z ≤ 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as a recalled result from McCoy [12]. A useful way to see the inequality is via the edge-energy formula. Choose an integer representative z = ∑ n_v v and let x be represented by the indicator function ε_v of R. For each oriented edge ab, the contribution to (x − z)·z is ((ε_a − ε_b) − (n_a − n_b))(n_a − n_b). If the edge is internal to R or to V \ R, then ε_a − ε_b = 0 and the contribution is −(n_a − n_b)^2 ≤ 0. If the edge crosses the cut, then ε_a − ε_b = ±1, and the contribution is (1 − m)m or (−1 − m)m for an integer m = n_a − n_b, both of which are nonpositive. Summing over all edges gives the claimed inequality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Λ(G) be the graph lattice of a finite connected graph G. A nonzero vector x ∈ Λ(G) is irreducible, meaning that there do not exist nonzero y,z ∈ Λ(G) with x = y + z and y·z ≥ 0, if and only if x = [R] for some subset R ⊆ V such that the subgraph induced by R and the subgraph induced by V \ R are both connected.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper recalls this from McCoy [12]. The forward direction is based on the fact that if either R or V \ R is disconnected, then [R], or equivalently −[V \ R], can be split as a sum over disconnected components with dot product 0, giving a reducible decomposition. Conversely, if R and V \ R are connected, then for any decomposition [R] = z + ([R] − z), Lemma 3.1 gives ([R] − z)·z ≤ 0. If equality occurs, the edgewise proof of Lemma 3.1 forces z to be constant on each of the two connected induced subgraphs and to have only the possible cut jump compatible with [R]; in the quotient by [V], this means z is either 0 or [R]. Hence no nontrivial decomposition with nonnegative cross product exists.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a finite connected graph G, the following are equivalent: (i) G is 2-connected, i.e. G cannot be disconnected by deleting a single vertex; (ii) every vertex v ∈ V, viewed as an element of the graph lattice Λ(G), is irreducible; (iii) the lattice Λ(G) is indecomposable, i.e. it cannot be written as an orthogonal direct sum Λ(G) = L1 ⊕ L2 with L1 and L2 nonzero sublattices.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper recalls this from McCoy [12]. The equivalence of (i) and (ii) follows directly from Lemma 3.2: the vertex v is represented by [{v}], whose support is connected, and its complement is connected exactly when deleting v does not disconnect G. The equivalence with indecomposability expresses the same cut-vertex obstruction at the lattice level: a cut vertex produces an orthogonal splitting associated to the components separated by that vertex, while any nontrivial orthogonal splitting of the graph lattice detects such a separation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a 2-connected finite graph with no cut-edges, and let Λ(G) be its graph lattice. Suppose there is a vertex v ∈ V and elements x,y ∈ Λ(G) such that v = x + y and x·y = −1. Then there is a cut edge e in the graph G \ {v}. If R and S are the vertex sets of the two components of (G \ {v}) \ {e}, then {x,y} = {[R] + v, [S] + v}. Furthermore, there are unique vertices u1,u2 ≠ v with x·u1 = 1 and y·u2 = 1, and every vertex w ∉ {v,u1,u2} satisfies w·x ≤ 0 and w·y ≤ 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as a recalled result from McCoy [12]. Its role is to convert a nearly reducible decomposition of a graph-lattice vertex, namely v = x + y with x·y = −1, into an explicit graph separation: deleting v creates a graph in which a single cut edge e separates two vertex sets R and S, and the summands are the corresponding cut vectors [R] + v and [S] + v. The uniqueness of u1 and u2 identifies the endpoints of the relevant separation by the only positive pairings of x and y with other vertices; all other vertex pairings are nonpositive.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let D be a reduced alternating link diagram, let Γ_D be its white graph for the chessboard coloring with all crossing incidence numbers −1, and identify the white Goeritz lattice Λ_D with the graph lattice of Γ_D. Suppose Γ_D is 2-connected and has a vertex v that can be written in Λ_D as v = x + y with x·y = −1. Then there are unique vertices u1,u2 ≠ v satisfying u1·x > 0 and u2·y > 0. Moreover, there is a flype from D to another reduced alternating diagram D′ and an isomorphism Λ_{D′} ≅ Λ_D such that the vertices of Γ_{D′} correspond to the vertices of Γ_D after replacing v,u1,u2 by x,y,u1+u2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites McCoy [12] for the proof. Algebraically, Lemma 3.4 identifies a cut edge in Γ_D \ {v} and determines the two summands x and y from the separated vertex sets. Diagrammatically, this cut-edge configuration is exactly the white-graph trace of a flypable alternating tangle. Performing the flype changes the white graph by replacing the old regions v,u1,u2 with regions represented in the lattice by x,y,u1+u2, while preserving the Goeritz lattice up to the stated isomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4 cut-set flype observation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let D be an alternating diagram with white graph Γ_D. Suppose two white regions v and w form a cut set in Γ_D and that there is an edge between v and w. If G1 is a component of Γ_D \ {v,w} adjacent in the plane to an edge between v and w, then a flype rotates the tangle corresponding to G1. If D′ is the result, then Λ_{D′} is naturally isomorphic to Λ_D by replacing each vertex z ∈ G1 with −z and replacing v and w with v + [G1] and w + [G1], respectively.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper explains this directly from the planar flype picture. The key algebraic identity is that for every z ∈ G1 one has (v + w)·z = −z·[G1]. This identity makes the stated replacements preserve all pairings among the white-region vectors after the tangle corresponding to G1 is rotated. Thus the flype has a transparent lattice description in terms of adding the component sum [G1] to the two boundary regions and negating the interior region vectors.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 6.2 and Section 6.1 setup for p/q-changemaker lattices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For q ≥ 2 and p/q > 1, write p/q = n − r/q with 0 < r < q, and write the negative continued fraction p/q = [a0,a1,...,al]_- with a0 = n and ai ≥ 2 for i ≥ 1. Define m0 = 0 and mk = ∑_{i=1}^k ai − k for 1 ≤ k ≤ l, and put s = ml. Let f1,...,ft,e0,...,es be an orthonormal basis for Z^{t+s+1}. Let w0 = e0 + σ1 f1 + ... + σt ft, where ∥w0∥^2 = n and the nondecreasing sequence σ1,...,σt satisfies the changemaker condition 0 ≤ σ1 ≤ 1 and σ_{i−1} ≤ σ_i ≤ σ1 + ... + σ_{i−1} + 1 for 1 < i ≤ t. For 1 ≤ k ≤ l, set wk = −e_{m_{k−1}} + e_{m_{k−1}+1} + ... + e_{mk}. The lattice L = ⟨w0,...,wl⟩^⊥ ⊆ Z^{t+s+1} is called a p/q-changemaker lattice. Its fractional part is LF = ⟨w1,...,wl⟩^⊥ ∩ ⟨e0,...,es⟩, and its integer part is LI = ⟨w0⟩^⊥ ∩ ⟨e0,f1,...,ft⟩. For x ∈ L write x0 = x·e0; one decomposes x as x = xI + xF − x0 e0 with xI ∈ LI and xF ∈ LF, and for x,y ∈ L one has x·y = xI·yI − x0 y0 + xF·yF. The fractional part LF has a basis v0,...,vm constructed from the indices not among m0,...,ml, with Gram matrix satisfying vi·vj = ∥vi∥^2 if i = j, vi·vj = −1 if |i−j| = 1, and vi·vj = 0 if |i−j| > 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is mostly definition and direct coordinate calculation. The vectors wk have the continued-fraction intersection pattern wk·wk = ak, wk·w_{k+1} = −1, and wk·wj = 0 for |k−j| > 1. The decomposition x = xI + xF − x0 e0 separates the f-coordinate part and the e-coordinate part while correcting for the shared e0-coordinate; because the two coordinate blocks otherwise have disjoint support, the dot-product formula follows immediately. The basis v0,...,vm of LF is built so that consecutive basis vectors overlap in exactly one e-coordinate with opposite signs, giving pairing −1, while nonconsecutive vectors have disjoint support.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.4 (Brown complete-sequence criterion)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let σ = (σ1,...,σs) be a nondecreasing sequence of integers. There is a subset A ⊆ {1,...,s} with k = ∑_{i∈A} σ_i for every integer k satisfying 0 ≤ k ≤ σ1 + ... + σs if and only if σ satisfies the changemaker condition 0 ≤ σ1 ≤ 1 and σ_{i−1} ≤ σ_i ≤ σ1 + ... + σ_{i−1} + 1 for all 1 < i ≤ s.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this theorem of Brown [1]. It is used as a black-box subset-sum device: whenever a coefficient σ_g is bounded by the changemaker condition, one can choose a subset of earlier coefficients summing to σ_g or to σ_g − 1, depending on the required test vector. These subset choices are the source of many vectors of the form −f_g plus a controlled sum of earlier f_i coordinates.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L = ⟨w0,...,wl⟩^⊥ ⊆ Z^{t+s+1} be a p/q-changemaker lattice in the q ≥ 2 setup, with w0 = e0 + σ1 f1 + ... + σt ft. Assume σ_i ≥ 1 for every i. Suppose z ∈ L has the coordinate form z = −f_k + ∑_{i∈A} f_i + ∑_{j∈B} e_j, where A ⊆ {1,...,k−1} and B ⊆ {0,...,s}. Then z is irreducible in L.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume z = x + y with x,y ∈ L and x·y ≥ 0. In each orthonormal coordinate f_i or e_j, the two integer coordinates of x and y sum to −1, 0, or 1, because z has only coefficients in {−1,0,1}. For integer pairs with such a sum, the product of the two coordinates is always ≤ 0. Since x·y is the sum of all these coordinate products and is assumed nonnegative, every coordinate product must be 0. Thus the support of z is partitioned between x and y coordinate by coordinate. After possibly interchanging x and y, assume x·f_k = −1. Then every coordinate of y in the basis f_i,e_j is nonnegative. The condition y ∈ L gives y·w0 = y·e0 + ∑ σ_i(y·f_i) = 0, and because all terms are nonnegative and all σ_i ≥ 1, this forces y·e0 = 0 and y·f_i = 0 for all i. Orthogonality to w1,w2,... then recursively forces all e_j-coordinates of y to vanish. Hence y = 0, so no nontrivial decomposition exists.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the fractional part LF of a p/q-changemaker lattice, with basis v0,...,vm as in Section 6.1, a vector xF ∈ LF is irreducible if and only if xF = ±(v_a + v_{a+1} + ... + v_b) for some 0 ≤ a ≤ b ≤ m.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper identifies LF with the graph lattice of a connected graph whose vertices are v0,...,vm and w = −(v0 + ... + vm). The vertices v0,...,vm span a path, and the endpoints v0 and vm are connected to w; additional edges to w encode any larger diagonal norms. Lemma 3.2 then characterizes irreducibles as sums over vertex subsets whose induced subgraph and complement are connected. Up to replacing a subset by its complement, one may assume w is not in the subset. A connected subset of the path on v0,...,vm is exactly a contiguous interval {v_a,...,v_b}, and its complement remains connected because the path ends connect through w. Therefore the irreducibles are precisely the signed interval sums.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a p/q-changemaker lattice with q ≥ 2, and write each x ∈ L as x = xI + xF − x0 e0 with xI ∈ LI, xF ∈ LF, and x0 = x·e0. If x ∈ L is irreducible, then its fractional component xF ∈ LF is irreducible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If x0 = 0, then xI and xF both lie in L and xI·xF = 0. Since x = xI + xF is irreducible, one of these summands must be zero, so the conclusion is immediate in the convention used in the paper. Now assume x0 ≠ 0 and replace x by −x if necessary so x0 > 0. Write xF = ∑ c_i v_i with c0 = x0, and put c_{m+1} = 0. Choose gF minimal with c_{gF+1} ≤ 0, and set zF = v0 + ... + v_{gF}. Lemma 6.6 says zF is irreducible, and a direct computation using the chain Gram matrix gives (xF − zF)·zF = (∥v0∥^2−1)(c0−1) + ∑_{i=1}^{gF−1}(∥vi∥^2−2)(c_i−1) + (∥v_{gF}∥^2−1)(c_{gF}−1) − c_{gF+1} ≥ x0 − 1. Next choose gI minimal with x·f_{gI} ≤ 0. By Brown's criterion there is A ⊆ {1,...,gI−1} with σ_{gI} − 1 = ∑_{i∈A} σ_i, so zI = −f_{gI} + e0 + ∑_{i∈A} f_i lies in LI. Minimality of gI gives (xI − zI)·zI = −x·f_{gI} − 1 + ∑_{i∈A}(x·f_i − 1) + (x0 − 1) ≥ x0 − 2. Put z = zI + zF − e0 ∈ L. Using the dot-product decomposition, (x − z)·z = (xI − zI)·zI − (x0 − 1) + (xF − zF)·zF ≥ x0 − 2 ≥ −1. If this product is nonnegative, irreducibility forces x = z. If it equals −1, then necessarily x0 = 1, and with z′ = xI + zF − e0 one has (x − z′)·z′ = (xF − zF)·zF ≥ 0, so irreducibility forces x = z′. In both cases xF = zF, an interval sum, hence irreducible by Lemma 6.6.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a p/q-changemaker lattice with q ≥ 2 and w0 = e0 + σ1 f1 + ... + σt ft. The following are equivalent: (i) L is indecomposable as an orthogonal direct sum; (ii) σ_i ≥ 1 for every 1 ≤ i ≤ t; (iii) L contains no vector of norm 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For any i, the basis vector f_i lies in L exactly when σ_i = 0, because f_i is orthogonal to all w_j for j ≥ 1 and f_i·w0 = σ_i. No e_j lies in L, since some w_k has nonzero pairing with e_j. Thus norm-one vectors in L occur exactly as f_i with σ_i = 0, proving the equivalence of (ii) and (iii). If σ_i = 0, then ⟨f_i⟩ splits off orthogonally from L, so indecomposability implies (ii). Conversely assume all σ_i ≥ 1. For each k, Brown's criterion gives either σ_k = 1 + σ1 + ... + σ_{k−1}, in which case define u_k = −f_k + f_{k−1} + ... + f_1 + v0, or σ_k = ∑_{i∈A_k} σ_i for some A_k ⊆ {1,...,k−1}, in which case define u_k = −f_k + ∑_{i∈A_k} f_i. Then B = {u1,...,ut,v1,...,vm} is a basis for L. Lemmas 6.5 and 6.6 show every element of B is irreducible. If L = L1 ⊕ L2 were a nontrivial orthogonal splitting, every irreducible basis element would have to lie entirely in L1 or entirely in L2; otherwise its projections would give a nontrivial orthogonal decomposition. Since u1 = −f1 + v0, place u1 in L1. The pairings v1·u1 = −1 and vi·v_{i+1} = −1 force all vi into L1. Then for k > 1, if u_k contains v0, one has u1·u_k = ∥v0∥^2 − 1 ≥ 1, forcing u_k into L1; otherwise, with l = min A_k, one has u_k·u_l = −1 and induction forces u_k into L1. Hence all of B lies in L1, so L = L1 and the splitting was trivial.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 7 preliminary observation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let D be a reduced alternating diagram, and suppose its white Goeritz lattice Λ_D is isomorphic to a p/q-changemaker lattice L with p > q ≥ 2. Let V_D denote the set of vectors in L corresponding to the vertices of the white graph Γ_D under a fixed lattice isomorphism. Since D is reduced, Γ_D has no self-loops or cut-edges, and Λ_D has no norm-one vectors. Therefore Lemma 6.8 implies Λ_D is indecomposable, and Lemma 3.3 implies Γ_D is 2-connected and every vertex vector in V_D is irreducible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a direct chain of previously stated facts. Reduced alternating diagrams have white graphs without the degeneracies that would create norm-one vectors in the graph lattice. Under the changemaker identification, absence of norm-one vectors implies, by Lemma 6.8, that the changemaker lattice is indecomposable. Applying Lemma 3.3 to the graph lattice Λ(Γ_D) then shows Γ_D is 2-connected and all of its vertex classes are irreducible.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setup where a reduced alternating diagram D has white lattice Λ_D identified with a p/q-changemaker lattice L and where v0,...,vm is the fractional-part basis of LF, one may perform a sequence of flypes to obtain a diagram in which v1,...,vm are all vertices of the white graph, i.e. v1,...,vm ∈ V_D for the resulting diagram.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose c maximal with 1 ≤ c ≤ m such that v_c is not currently a vertex. By Lemma 6.6, v_c is irreducible in LF and hence, in the graph-lattice setting, Lemma 3.2 gives a subset R of current vertices with v_c = ∑_{x∈R} x. Therefore some current vertex u has fractional part uF = v_a + ... + v_b with a ≤ c ≤ b. Since v_c itself is not a vertex, u ≠ v_c, so a < b. The pairing v_b·u = v_b·uF = ∥v_b∥^2 − 1 is positive, which is impossible if v_b were a distinct current vertex because distinct graph vertices have nonpositive pairing. By maximality of c, this forces b = c. Then (u − v_c)·v_c = v_{c−1}·v_c = −1, so Lemma 4.1 applies and a flype replaces u by v_c among the vertex vectors. The already established vertices v_{c+1},...,vm remain vertices because v_c has nonpositive pairing with them except at its own place. Iterating decreases c and eventually makes every v1,...,vm a vertex.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: After applying Lemma 7.2 so that v1,...,vm ∈ V_D, there is a unique vertex v ∈ V_D with v·e0 > 0, and it satisfies vF = v0. There is also a unique vertex w ∈ V_D with w·e0 < 0, and it satisfies wF = −(v0 + ... + vm).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let v be any vertex with v·e0 > 0. Lemma 6.7 says vF is irreducible in LF, so by Lemma 6.6 it has the form v0 + ... + v_b. If b > 0, then v_b is already a vertex and v_b·v = v_b·vF > 0, impossible for two distinct graph vertices. Thus vF = v0. Similarly, any vertex w with w·e0 < 0 has wF = −(v0 + ... + v_c); if c < m, then the vertex v_{c+1} has w·v_{c+1} = 1, again impossible, so wF = −(v0 + ... + vm). To prove uniqueness of the positive vertex, suppose u1 and u2 both have fractional part v0. If they are distinct, then U = u1 + u2 is a sum of distinct vertices and UF = 2v0. Let g be minimal with U·f_g ≤ 0. Brown's criterion gives A ⊆ {1,...,g−1} with σ_g − 1 = ∑_{i∈A} σ_i, and z = −f_g + v0 + ∑_{i∈A} f_i lies in L. Then (U − z)·z = −U·f_g − 1 + ∑_{i∈A}(U·f_i − 1) + ∥v0∥^2 ≥ −1 + ∥v0∥^2 > 0, contradicting Lemma 3.1 for the sum U of distinct vertices. Hence u1 = u2. Since the total sum of all graph vertices is zero in the graph lattice, pairing with e0 then also gives uniqueness of the negative vertex.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the same alternating-diagram/changemaker setup, let x = ∑_{u∈R} u be a sum of vertex vectors over some subset R ⊆ V_D. Then |x·f1| ≤ 2. Furthermore, if x is irreducible and x·e0 ≠ 0, then |x·f1| ≤ 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By replacing x by −x if needed, assume x·f1 ≥ 0. For the first bound, choose g > 1 minimal with x·f_g ≤ 0. Brown's criterion gives A ⊆ {1,...,g−1} with σ_g − 1 = ∑_{i∈A} σ_i, and z = −f_g + f1 + ∑_{i∈A} f_i lies in L. Let ε = z·f1, so ε is 1 or 2 depending on whether 1 ∉ A or 1 ∈ A. Lemma 3.1 applied to the vertex sum x gives 0 ≥ (x − z)·z = −x·f_g − 1 + ∑_{i∈A \ {1}}(x·f_i − 1) + ε(x·f1 − ε) ≥ −1 + ε(x·f1 − ε). Hence x·f1 ≤ ε + 1/ε ≤ 5/2, and integrality gives x·f1 ≤ 2. For the irreducible refinement, assume x·e0 > 0; the negative case follows by replacing x by −x. Lemmas 6.7 and 6.6 imply x·e0 = 1. Let g be minimal with x·f_g ≤ 0. If g = 1, take z = −f1 + xF. Irreducibility of x forces either x = z or (x − z)·z ≤ −1; the displayed calculation gives the desired bound. If g > 1, choose A as before. If 1 ∈ A, set z = −f_g + xF + ∑_{i∈A} f_i; if 1 ∉ A, set z = −f_g + f1 + ∑_{i∈A} f_i. Then z ∈ L and z·f1 = 1. Irreducibility gives either x = z or −1 ≥ (x − z)·z ≥ x·f1 − 2, forcing x·f1 ≤ 1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let v and w be the unique vertices from Lemma 7.3, so vF = v0 and wF = −(v0 + ... + vm). Then v·w ≤ vF·wF + 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 6.5, z = −f1 + v0 is irreducible. Lemma 3.2 therefore represents z as a sum of vertices. Comparing e0-coordinates shows that this sum contains v and not w, so z − v + w is also a sum of vertices. Lemma 7.4 applied to this sum gives (z − v + w)·f1 = −1 − v·f1 + w·f1 ≥ −2, so either v·f1 ≤ 0 or w·f1 ≥ 0. Treat the case v·f1 ≤ 0; the other is analogous using z = f1 − (v0 + ... + vm). Lemma 7.4 gives v·f1 ∈ {0,−1}. If v·f1 = −1, then irreducibility forces v = z, and v·w = −w·f1 + vF·wF ≤ 1 + vF·wF. If v·f1 = 0, then (v − z)·z = −1 and v·w = (v − z)·w − w·f1 + vF·wF. Lemma 3.4 gives (v − z)·w ≤ 1, and Lemma 7.4 gives w·f1 ≥ −1; it remains only to rule out the simultaneous worst case. If (v − z)·w = 1, Lemma 3.4 produces a vertex u not among v,w with u·z = 1 and u + w irreducible. The equality u·z = 1 implies u has zero fractional part and u·f1 = −1. Applying Lemma 7.4 to the irreducible vector u + w gives −1 + w·f1 ≥ −1, hence w·f1 ≥ 0, completing the bound.

proof_techniques.name: Graph-lattice cut-vector calculus
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the graph lattice Λ(G), where a subset R of vertices gives a cut vector [R]. The key inequality is Lemma 3.1: for any lattice vector z, ([R] − z)·z ≤ 0. This turns arbitrary decompositions of [R] into edgewise constraints and is the basis for the characterization of irreducible graph-lattice elements as exactly those [R] for which R and its complement are connected.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a weighted-tree lattice can be compared to, embedded in, or reduced to a graph-lattice or cut-lattice situation, this gives a direct way to prove irreducibility of selected vertex sums. The connected-support criterion suggests looking for a vertex or branch whose removal leaves a connected complement in an auxiliary graph.

proof_techniques.name: Certifying irreducibility by coordinate sign splitting
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 6.5 proves irreducibility for vectors with one negative coordinate and all other nonzero coordinates equal to +1 in an orthonormal ambient lattice. If z = x + y and x·y ≥ 0, then because the coordinates of z are in {−1,0,1}, every coordinate product (x_i y_i) is ≤ 0; the nonnegative total pairing forces every product to be zero. Thus one summand receives a nonnegative coordinate vector. Orthogonality to a positive vector w0, together with recursive orthogonality to the plumbing-chain vectors wj, forces that summand to vanish.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a robust template for proving a vertex is irreducible after finding a convenient coordinate model: show any nonnegative-pairing split must separate coordinates, then use positivity/orthogonality constraints to kill one summand. It may be useful if the weighted tree lattice is embedded into a diagonal lattice or admits a basis with similar {−1,0,1} vertex coordinates.

proof_techniques.name: Brown changemaker subsets as test-vector constructors
proof_techniques.description: The changemaker condition is used through Brown's theorem to build subsets A of earlier indices with prescribed coefficient sums, such as ∑_{i∈A} σ_i = σ_g − 1. This allows construction of special lattice vectors like −f_g + e0 + ∑_{i∈A} f_i or −f_g + f1 + ∑_{i∈A} f_i. Pairing these test vectors with a suspected sum of vertices and applying Lemma 3.1 yields strong coefficient bounds.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In an analogous weighted-tree setting, one can look for positive linear relations among coordinates or weights that let one build low-norm test vectors. Such vectors can be paired against hypothetical decompositions to force inequalities contradicting reducibility.

proof_techniques.name: Indecomposability from an irreducible connected basis
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 6.8 proves indecomposability by constructing an explicit basis B of irreducible elements and showing the graph of nonzero pairings among basis elements is connected. In any orthogonal splitting L = L1 ⊕ L2, an irreducible element must lie wholly in one summand; nonzero pairings then force adjacent basis elements into the same summand, and connectivity forces the whole basis into one summand.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A solver could try to build a basis or spanning set of irreducible candidates in the tree lattice and connect them by nonzero pairings. Even if the final goal is only one irreducible vertex, proving a stronger indecomposability statement for a sublattice may force the existence of such a vertex.

proof_techniques.name: Projection of irreducibility to a fractional part
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 6.7 decomposes a changemaker lattice vector x into integer and fractional components xI and xF with a correction in the shared e0-coordinate. The proof constructs interval vectors zF in the fractional chain and matching integer test vectors zI; inequalities show that if x is irreducible, then xF must equal a single interval sum. This transfers irreducibility information from the full lattice to a simpler chain-like lattice LF where irreducibles are classified.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a weighted tree, one might decompose the lattice along branches emanating from a distinguished vertex and try to prove that irreducibility or reducibility of a vertex is controlled by its projection to a branch lattice. The paper's method shows how to combine branch inequalities with a central coordinate correction.

proof_techniques.name: Algebraic graph modification from a near-splitting
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.4 and Lemma 4.1 analyze the special situation where a graph-lattice vertex v decomposes as v = x + y with x·y = −1. Rather than being an arbitrary decomposition, it comes from a precise cut-edge in G \ {v}; in alternating diagrams this corresponds to a flype that replaces the vertex data v,u1,u2 by x,y,u1+u2 while preserving the lattice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a vertex of the weighted tree is reducible or nearly reducible, an analogous structural description of the decomposition might permit induction or local modification of the tree. The idea is to turn algebraic splittings into graph-theoretic separations.

proof_techniques.name: Coefficient bounds for sums of vertices
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 7.4 bounds |x·f1| for any x that is a sum of graph vertices, and improves the bound when x is irreducible with nonzero e0-coordinate. The method is to choose a changemaker test vector z and apply the universal graph-lattice inequality (x − z)·z ≤ 0; in the irreducible case, the dichotomy that either x = z or (x − z)·z must be negative sharpens the estimate.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original problem, if one can identify a coordinate or linear functional measuring the defect w(v) − d(v), similar coefficient bounds may limit how all vertices could decompose and force at least one vertex to be irreducible.

proof_techniques.name: Using absence of norm-one vectors to force graph vertices irreducible
proof_techniques.description: In Section 7, a reduced alternating diagram with changemaker lattice has no norm-one vectors. Lemma 6.8 converts this into indecomposability of the changemaker lattice, and Lemma 3.3 then converts indecomposability of the graph lattice into 2-connectedness of the white graph and irreducibility of every graph vertex.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original lattice is positive definite, and if one can rule out norm-one summands or orthogonal decompositions in a suitable sublattice, this chain suggests a route from indecomposability to existence of irreducible vertex elements.

other_useful_info: The paper states that all graph-lattice results in Section 3, including Lemmas 3.1–3.4, are recalled from McCoy [12], where complete proofs are available. Brown's subset-sum criterion for changemaker sequences is cited as Brown [1] and is the combinatorial engine behind the test-vector constructions. For reduced alternating diagrams, the white Goeritz lattice is identified with a graph lattice when the white graph has no self-loops or cut-edges; flypes preserve the lattice while changing the distinguished vertex set. The most reusable structural chain in the paper is: no norm-one vectors in a changemaker lattice implies indecomposable; indecomposable graph lattice implies 2-connected graph; 2-connected graph implies all graph vertices are irreducible.
why_relevant: Applies generalized changemaker lattices to alternating-link Goeritz lattices. Its structural arguments about lattices that are simultaneously graph-like and changemaker-like are relevant to proving irreducibility statements for vertices.
